current_fact.fact_text: On April 9, 1990, the Sahtu Dene and Métis Comprehensive Land Claim Agreement was signed, recognizing Indigenous title to 180,000 square kilometers of ancestral territory in Canada’s Mackenzie Valley. Instead of a map drawn in a capital city, this agreement acknowledged a landscape already mapped in stories, trails, and subsistence routes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

current_fact.match_reason: An episode centered on Indigenous desert peoples, land, and resistance harmonizes with a northern land-claims victory about relationship to territory and survival.

queue.fact_text: On April 9, 2009, up to 60,000 people filled the streets of Tbilisi, Georgia, demanding the resignation of President Mikheil Saakashvili. For weeks, demonstrators turned boulevards into open-air forums, using posters, megaphones, and sheer presence to test how far post-Soviet democracy could stretch.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A fiercely political, protest-focused episode echoes the energy of mass demonstrations challenging a sitting government in Tbilisi.

queue.fact_text: On April 9, 2013, a magnitude 6.3 earthquake struck near Bushehr in Iran, killing dozens and injuring hundreds while shaking homes built on layers of ancient fault lines. In less than a minute, everyday rooms turned into falling geometry, and centuries of quiet tectonic stress released in a single jolt.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Trip-hop’s deep, shifting undercurrents and sudden drops provide a sonic metaphor for tectonic plates building quiet tension and then releasing it in an earthquake.

queue.fact_text: On April 9, 2017, passenger Dr. David Dao was violently dragged from an overbooked United Express flight, and the viral video sparked global outrage over airline practices. In hours, a small moment inside a narrow cabin became a worldwide debate about power, dignity, and what happens when policy ignores people.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A show about our entanglement with technology and viral moments aligns with an incident amplified and reshaped entirely by smartphones and social media.

queue.fact_text: On April 9, 2009, Iran’s nuclear program was also in the headlines, but years earlier, on this same date in 1959, Project Mercury was announced; decades later, the same mix of fear and possibility echoes in nuclear energy. From the Atomic Energy Commission to modern debates, April 9 carries a quiet thread about who controls the atom and why.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A sprawling, exploratory set about journeys and perception complements the long, complex voyage from early atomic commissions to global nuclear debates.

queue.fact_text: On April 9, 2013, while the earth shook in Iran, on other April 9ths artists were shaking sound itself—like Édouard-Léon Scott’s 1860 recording and Marian Anderson’s 1939 open-air recital—reminding us that this date keeps returning to the theme of voices captured, silenced, and amplified. Each era leaves its vibrations: in glass, on tape, online.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Sedna FM’s own ‘where it all began’ episode mirrors a meta-fact about April 9 as a recurring starting point for new ways of recording and projecting human voices.

queue.fact_text: On many April 9ths, from the Journey of Reconciliation to Georgian street protests, public space has turned into a stage where ordinary people perform extraordinary courage. Sidewalks and bus seats became lines of poetry written with bodies instead of ink.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: An episode about the dancefloor as a place to claim space and power resonates with streets and buses turned into stages for protest and collective presence.

queue.fact_text: Across different centuries, April 9 keeps looping through themes of travel—by shuttle, ship, bus, and boot—and of who gets to move freely. From segregated bus rides to overbooked flights, from submarines colliding in secret to land rights reclaimed, it quietly asks the same question: whose journey counts?

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A travel-ready, motion-focused mix is a natural soundtrack for a reflective meta-fact about April 9 as a day of contested, celebrated, and transformed journeys.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1959, NASA revealed the "Mercury Seven"—the first group of American astronauts—turning cold-war test pilots into global symbols of human curiosity. Behind the heroic portraits was a crash‑course in inventing how humans would live, work, and even think in space, long before anyone had tried it.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: An episode about rethinking empty space mirrors the Mercury program’s leap into the unknown and the mental shift needed to imagine humans in orbit.

published.fact_text: Space Shuttle Endeavour lifted off on the STS-59 mission on April 9, 1994, carrying a powerful radar system to map Earth in 3D from orbit. Instead of looking outward, this spaceflight turned its gaze back home, revealing hidden patterns in forests, cities, and mountains beneath the clouds.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A slowly expanding, global-sounding mix echoes a shuttle mission that gradually revealed the textured, hidden landscapes of Earth from above.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1860, Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville used his phonautograph to capture the first known recording of an audible human voice—seventeen seconds of someone singing "Au clair de la lune." The sound couldn’t be played back for over a century, lingering as ink waves on smoked glass until modern scientists resurrected the ghostly voice in 2008.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: An episode about vocal technology and the evolution of recorded voice pairs perfectly with the very first human voice ever captured on a machine.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1939, contralto Marian Anderson sang to over 75,000 people at the Lincoln Memorial after being barred from Constitution Hall because she was Black. Her open-air concert turned a denial into a historic act of resistance, weaving classical arias and spirituals into a quiet demand for dignity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: A show honoring powerful female voices and their cultural impact reflects Marian Anderson’s landmark performance as both art and civil-rights statement.

published.fact_text: The Journey of Reconciliation set off on April 9, 1947, as an interracial group of riders boarded buses through the upper South to peacefully challenge segregated seating. Their quiet insistence on sitting together turned Greyhound aisles into moving protest lines and laid the groundwork for the famed Freedom Rides of the 1960s.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: A hip-hop episode about using voice and courage to confront injustice mirrors the riders’ deliberate challenge to segregation through everyday journeys.

published.fact_text: From April 9–11, 1947, a devastating tornado outbreak known as the Glazier–Higgins–Woodward tornadoes tore across Texas, Oklahoma, and Kansas, killing 181 people. One of the twisters grew to over 1.6 km wide, erasing entire blocks and becoming a case study in the raw, rotating energy of the atmosphere.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The episode’s theme of raw electrical energy and shock parallels the sudden, immense power of the tornado outbreak ripping across the plains.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1682, René-Robert Cavelier de La Salle reached the mouth of the Mississippi River and ceremonially claimed the vast watershed for France, naming it Louisiana. Without satellite maps, his journey stitched together Indigenous trails, river currents, and guesswork to redraw the mental map of an entire continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A seafaring, river-and-coastline themed episode matches La Salle’s long waterborne expedition to the Mississippi’s end and the naming of Louisiana.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1917, Canadian troops launched their assault on Vimy Ridge as part of the Battle of Arras, capturing a heavily fortified high point that had resisted previous attacks. The meticulously planned nighttime tunnels, creeping artillery barrages, and synchronized advance turned a brutal hillside into a defining moment of Canadian identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

published.match_reason: An episode about evolution and hard-won transformation mirrors how careful preparation and sacrifice at Vimy Ridge reshaped both tactics and national identity.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1918, the Battle of the Lys began, and the Portuguese Expeditionary Corps was overwhelmed during Germany’s Spring Offensive in Flanders. Exhausted troops, thin supply lines, and a surprise pre‑dawn bombardment turned their sector into one of the war’s most devastating collapses.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A reflective episode about enduring storms and emerging with cautious hope suits a battle defined by collapse, hardship, and the need to rebuild after loss.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1940, Germany launched Operation Weserübung, simultaneously invading Denmark and Norway in a surprise strike that combined naval forces, paratroopers, and diplomacy turned on its head. Denmark capitulated in hours, while Norway became a long, icy chessboard of fjords, saboteurs, and supply lines.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: The hard, driving energy of a rock-focused episode pairs with the sudden, forceful nature of a blitzkrieg-style invasion reshaping Northern Europe overnight.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1942, Japan’s 1st Air Fleet launched a sweeping raid across the Indian Ocean, sinking the British carrier HMS Hermes and the Australian destroyer HMAS Vampire off Sri Lanka. For a few weeks, the Japanese navy projected power astonishingly far from home waters, briefly turning the Indian Ocean into another frontline.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A sea-voyage themed episode naturally resonates with a dramatic naval-air operation played out on open ocean, where ships and waves set the stage.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1945, the German heavy cruiser Admiral Scheer was destroyed in harbor by a massive Royal Air Force bombing raid on Kiel. The once-feared commerce raider, which had prowled the Atlantic and Arctic hunting Allied shipping, ended the war as a twisted hulk in shallow water.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: An open-form, drifting evening mix mirrors a warship’s long, wandering career ending in a final, explosive curtain call in harbor.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1945, the United States created the Atomic Energy Commission to oversee nuclear research and the development of atomic power in the wake of the Manhattan Project. This new body sat uneasily between military secrecy and civilian promise, charged with managing a technology that could light cities—or erase them.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: An episode about scientific learning, effort, and responsibility fits the birth of a commission meant to guide humanity’s most powerful new science.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1952, a Japan Air Lines DC-6 on Flight 301 slammed into Mount Mihara, an active volcano on Izu Ōshima island, killing all 37 people on board. Flying without modern terrain‑warning systems, the crew vanished into cloud and mist, and only later did rescuers find wreckage scattered across the volcano’s rugged flank.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A travel-and-Asia themed episode, with mountains and discipline in the background, offers a contemplative counterpoint to an early era of risky flight over a Japanese volcano.

published.fact_text: Also on April 9, 1952, the Bolivian National Revolution toppled the government of Hugo Ballivián and launched sweeping reforms, including universal suffrage, land redistribution, and the nationalization of tin mines. In a few years, a country built on rigid hierarchies tried to rewrite who had a voice and who owned the ground beneath their feet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A show about politically charged, socially conscious Afrobeat pairs with a revolution that redefined land, labor, and power for ordinary Bolivians.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1959, but beneath the sea rather than above it, the nuclear submarine USS George Washington would later become famous—yet on April 9, 1981, it accidentally rammed the Japanese cargo ship Nissho Maru, sinking it and killing two sailors. The incident highlighted how invisible, high‑tech vessels can still cause tragically old-fashioned maritime collisions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: An episode built around immersion and going below the surface reflects the hidden world of submarines and the unseen dangers of underwater navigation.

published.fact_text: On April 9, 1990, the Sahtu Dene and Métis Comprehensive Land Claim Agreement was signed, recognizing Indigenous title to 180,000 square kilometers of ancestral territory in Canada’s Mackenzie Valley. Instead of a map drawn in a capital city, this agreement acknowledged a landscape already mapped in stories, trails, and subsistence routes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An episode centered on Indigenous desert peoples, land, and resistance harmonizes with a northern land-claims victory about relationship to territory and survival.
